report:
## Advertising Segment Revenue Comparison: GOOGL vs. META

### Growth

Both GOOGL and META delivered strong top-line growth through 2025, though the breadth and pace of that growth differ in notable ways. GOOGL posted consistent double-digit expansion across geographies for the nine months ended September 30, 2025, with U.S. revenues up 13% and APAC revenues up 18% on a constant-currency basis [GOOGL:S2], while Google Cloud added $15.5 billion in incremental revenue year-over-year driven by infrastructure and platform services [GOOGL:S3]. GOOGL's advertising-anchored growth was complemented by subscription revenues from YouTube and Google One [GOOGL:S3], though foreign-currency volatility — including headwinds from U.S. dollar strength versus the Argentine peso during the nine-month interim period — remained a modest drag [GOOGL:S2]. META, meanwhile, recorded full-year 2025 advertising revenue of approximately $196 billion, reflecting robust monetization momentum across its Family of Apps platforms. Into Q1 2026, META showed further acceleration in select regions: Europe grew 39% and Rest of World grew 40% versus Q1 2025 [META:S3], though it is worth noting that U.S. & Canada and Asia-Pacific also grew meaningfully (each approximately 29%) in the same period — so the acceleration, while real, was somewhat more concentrated geographically than a headline reading might suggest. Both companies flagged unfavorable foreign-exchange impacts on reported revenue, a shared macro headwind rather than a company-specific vulnerability [GOOGL:S2][META:S4].

**GOOGL:** GOOGL delivered consistent double-digit growth across geographies for the nine months ended September 30, 2025, with U.S. revenues up 13% and APAC up 18% on a constant-currency basis [GOOGL:S2]. Google Cloud contributed an additional $15.5 billion in year-over-year revenue, driven by infrastructure and platform services [GOOGL:S3], and subscription revenues from YouTube and Google One added further diversification to the growth profile [GOOGL:S3]. Foreign-currency headwinds, including U.S. dollar strength versus the Argentine peso during the nine-month interim period, were a modest tempering factor [GOOGL:S2], and macroeconomic uncertainty remains a watch item heading into 2026 [GOOGL:S0].

**META:** META's full-year 2025 advertising revenue reached approximately $196 billion, reflecting strong and broad-based monetization across its platforms. Growth momentum carried into Q1 2026, with Europe up 39% and Rest of World up 40% year-over-year [META:S3]; U.S. & Canada and Asia-Pacific also posted solid gains of approximately 29% each in the same quarter, suggesting AI-driven ad improvements and expanding global reach are sustaining elevated growth rates across all regions, even if the magnitude varies. Unfavorable FX impacts were noted for full-year 2025 as well [META:S4], a headwind shared with GOOGL.

---

### Cross-Axis Takeaway

citations.claim: Both GOOGL and META delivered strong top-line growth through 2025, though META's acceleration into early 2026 appears particularly striking. META's total revenue reached roughly $200.97 billion for full-year 2025, with advertising revenue up 22% year-over-year and 'Other revenue' surging 50% [META:S1]; by Q1 2026, regional growth rates had accelerated sharply, with Europe up 39% and Rest of World up 40% versus the same period in 2025 [META:S3]. GOOGL also posted broad-based growth: U.S. revenues rose 13% and APAC revenues rose 18% on a constant-currency basis for the nine months ended September 30, 2025 [GOOGL:S2], while Google Cloud revenues increased $15.5 billion from 2024 to 2025, driven by infrastructure and platform services [GOOGL:S3]. GOOGL's growth was somewhat tempered by foreign-currency headwinds, particularly from U.S. dollar strength versus the Argentine peso [GOOGL:S2], whereas META similarly noted an unfavorable FX impact on full-year 2025 revenue [META:S4]. Overall, both companies are compounding at impressive rates, with META showing a notable re-acceleration in early 2026 and GOOGL demonstrating durable diversified growth anchored by Cloud.

quality.notes: The report is well-structured and readable, with clear geographic breakdowns and appropriate citation tagging. The comparison between GOOGL and META is substantive, and the report correctly flags nuance (e.g., geographic concentration of META's acceleration). Evidence is cited consistently with source tags. The report loses a point on clarity because the Growth section is redundant—the narrative intro paragraph largely repeats the same facts as the two company-specific sub-bullets, which adds length without adding insight. Comparison quality is strong but slightly hampered by the incomplete ending (the Cross-Axis Takeaway section is cut off), which prevents a full assessment of synthesis quality. Evidence coverage is solid for the metrics cited, though the report does not attempt to compare absolute advertising revenue scale between the two companies in a direct head-to-head table or statement, which would strengthen the comparison. Honesty on insufficient data is good—the report appropriately qualifies claims (e.g., 'somewhat more concentrated geographically than a headline reading might suggest') rather than overstating conclusions, and it flags FX as a shared macro factor rather than a company-specific weakness.
